options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance
s3.explain: A definition describes the essential nature of a condition as a deviation from normal.

It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s3.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s3.q: The incidence of this condition is highest in which population?
options: Depends on the specific condition and risk factors
s3.explain: Incidence varies by condition — each has its own epidemiology and risk profile.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

options: Delivery of fetus through vaginal route with instruments
Delivery of a viable fetus through an abdominal and uterine incision
Removal of the uterus through an abdominal incision
Incision of the perineum to facilitate delivery
s3.explain: CS is delivery of a viable fetus through an abdominal and uterine incision. It is the most common major operation performed globally.

s3.q: The incidence of cesarean section in Egypt approaches approximately:
s3.explain: Egypt has a CS rate approaching 70%, more than double the global average of about 30%.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:

s4.q: Which of the following is an absolute indication for cesarean section?
s4.explain: Absolute indications: extreme pelvic contraction, soft tissue obstruction, gross fetal anomalies, and major degree of placenta previa.

s4.q: The four major indications for CS accounting for >70% of operations include all EXCEPT:
s4.explain: The four major indications are: 1) Previous C-section, 2) Dystocia, 3) Malpresentation, 4) Suspected acute fetal compromise.

s4.q: A contraindication to cesarean section is:
s4.explain: Contraindications to CS include dead fetus and gross anomalies of the fetus (though these may be relative depending on context).

s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s5.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s5.q: The most commonly used skin incision for lower segment CS is:
s5.explain: Pfannenstiel incision (transverse suprapubic) is the most commonly used skin incision for lower segment CS. The uterine incision is closed in 2 layers.

s5.q: During lower segment CS, if the head is impacted in the pelvis, which forceps may be used for extraction?
s5.explain: If the head is impacted in the pelvis, it may be extracted by Wrigley's forceps through the uterine incision, or an assistant pushes the head upward by a hand in the vagina.

s6.q: Upper segment (classical) CS is indicated in which situation?
s6.explain: Indications for upper segment CS include placenta previa and accreta, extensive adhesions, large lower-segment fibroid, repaired fistulas, neglected shoulder presentation, planned hysterectomy, rapid delivery, and contraction ring.

s6.q: The uterine incision in upper segment CS is closed in how many layers?
s6.explain: The uterine incision in upper segment CS is closed in 3 layers: deep muscle, main bulk of muscle, and superficial muscle + peritoneum. Lower segment CS is closed in 2 layers.

s8.q: Which of the following is a complication of cesarean section?
options: Postpartum hemorrhage due to uterine artery injury
s8.explain: Complications include hemorrhage (mainly from uterine artery injury), bladder injury, anesthesia complications, shock, infection, paralytic ileus, thrombosis, adhesions, and scar rupture in subsequent pregnancies.

s8.q: Vaginal birth after CS (VBAC) is allowed ONLY for women who have had:
s8.explain: VBAC is only allowed for lower segment CS. No vaginal delivery trial is allowed for upper segment CS due to higher risk of uterine rupture (4% or more).

s9.q: Cesarean hysterectomy is indicated for all EXCEPT:
s9.explain: Indications include uncontrolled PPH, concealed accidental hemorrhage, operable cervical carcinoma, placenta accreta, multiple fibroids in older patients. Postmortem CS is emergency CS within 10 minutes of maternal death.

s10.q: During D&C, the cervix is gradually dilated up to which Hegar size?
s10.explain: During D&C, gradual dilatation of the cervix up to Hegar's 10 or more is performed before introducing the ovum forceps.

s11.q: Kielland's forceps are specifically used for:
options: Deep transverse arrest of the fetal head
s11.explain: Kielland's forceps have a minimal pelvic curve and sliding pivot, used for rotation in deep transverse arrest. Piper forceps are for after-coming head in breech.

s11.q: Which condition must be fulfilled before forceps application?
options: Fully dilated cervix and engaged head
s11.explain: Conditions include: anesthesia, adequate pelvic outlet, asepsis, empty bladder/bowel, uterine contractions, fully dilated cervix, engaged head in favorable position, and ruptured membranes.

s12.q: Which is a contraindication to vacuum extraction?
s12.explain: Contraindications include severe CPD, non-vertex presentation, premature fetus, and intact membranes. Fetal complications include cephalohematoma, scalp laceration, and rarely intracranial hemorrhage.

options: Is easier to repair than median
Has less risk of extension to anal sphincter
s13.explain: Mediolateral episiotomy requires more skill for repair but carries a lower risk of unintended extension to the anal sphincter compared to median episiotomy.
